title: Analysis: UK newspapers have already printed 63 editorials in 2026 backing North Sea drilling  - Carbon Brief
gist: UK right-leaning newspapers published 63 pro-North Sea-drilling editorials in the first half of 2026, more than double all of 2025, framing energy secretary Ed Miliband's net-zero policy as the cause of the energy crisis despite North Sea output having collapsed 75% since 2000 for structural reasons.

insights.claim: UK national newspapers published 63 editorials backing more North Sea oil and gas extraction in the first six months of 2026, more than double the number in all of 2025.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: An accelerating pro-drilling press campaign signals rising political pressure to reverse UK licensing policy, a swing factor for North Sea operators and UK energy strategy.
insights.quote: Just six months into the year, newspapers have already published 63 pro-North Sea editorials, according to analysis by Carbon Brief. This is more than double the number published in 2025

insights.claim: The Sun alone published 25 pro-North Sea editorials in the period while the Daily Telegraph and the Times each published 10, showing the campaign is concentrated in specific right-leaning titles.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Identifies which outlets are driving the pro-fossil narrative, useful for gauging where UK energy-policy lobbying pressure originates.
insights.locator: Right-leaning newspapers have led this campaign

insights.claim: The biggest surge came in March with 24 pro-drilling editorials as the Iran war escalated and a global energy crisis took shape, even though that crisis largely arose from the world's reliance on fossil fuels.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Geopolitical energy shocks are being used to reframe fossil expansion as security, a recurring pattern to watch whenever oil prices spike.

insights.claim: North Sea oil and gas extraction dropped 75% between 2000 and 2024 and roughly 90% of the fuels likely to be extracted there have already been burned, meaning output was on a clear downward trajectory long before Labour took power.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The mature-basin reality caps any realistic production upside from policy reversal, tempering bullish theses on UK domestic fossil supply.

insights.claim: Because UK oil and gas is sold by private companies on the open market at international rates, more North Sea drilling would have no impact on people's energy bills and very little effect on energy security, according to experts cited.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Undercuts the political premise that domestic drilling lowers UK prices; the real bill driver is gas-based power generation, pointing to renewables as the import-cutting lever.
insights.quote: trying to extract the last barrels of domestic oil and gas would have no impact on people’s energy bills and very little effect on energy security

insights.claim: The UK's high energy prices are primarily the result of its reliance on gas to generate electricity, and renewables and low-carbon technologies are expected to cut imports far more than new drilling could.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Reinforces that UK price relief and import reduction flow from renewable build-out, not drilling, informing where policy and capital should flow.
insights.quote: the UK’s high energy prices are primarily the result of its reliance on gas to generate electricity
insights.locator: In fact, the UK’s high energy prices

insights.claim: Nearly three-quarters of the 63 pro-drilling editorials also personally attacked Ed Miliband as a net-zero zealot on a green crusade, part of roughly 230 editorials criticising him in the two years since Labour won.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The concentration of attacks on one minister signals net-zero policy is becoming personally contested and potentially reversible under leadership change.
insights.quote: nearly three-quarters also attacked him as a “net-zero zealot” on a “green crusade”

insights.claim: Figures from ex-Labour PM Tony Blair to Reform UK's Nigel Farage, and even US president Donald Trump, have argued more drilling would boost the UK economy, with Trump partly blaming Keir Starmer's resignation on failing on North Sea oil.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Cross-spectrum political convergence on drilling, plus a leadership vacuum after Starmer, raises the odds of a UK net-zero policy softening.
insights.locator: Figures ranging from ex-Labour prime minister

insights.claim: Attacks on Miliband redoubled amid rumours he may be made chancellor under Andy Burnham, who is widely expected to become the next prime minister as the new Makerfield MP.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: A Burnham premiership with Miliband as chancellor would reshape UK fiscal and energy policy, a key political-risk scenario for UK-exposed investors.
insights.locator: These have redoubled in recent days

insights.claim: Right-leaning newspapers are backing the Conservative party as it frames itself as an anti-net-zero pro-fossil alternative, with the Daily Mail calling Kemi Badenoch's North Sea drilling proposal a concrete plan and the Sun urging drill, Kemi, drill.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Net-zero is being turned into a partisan wedge, making UK climate policy increasingly hostage to electoral swings.
insights.locator: Several of the newspapers have also thrown their support
